title: Rhubarb and almond cake
subtitle: A moist almond sponge with sharp, jammy rhubarb through the middle
excerpt: Ground almonds replace a third of the flour in this cake, making it unusually tender and moist. Rhubarb is macerated briefly before baking to draw off excess moisture, then pressed into the batter in pieces. The contrast between the sweet almond sponge and the sharp fruit is the whole point of the recipe. It bakes in about 50 minutes.
sourceNotes: Rhubarb and almond appear together in British baking from the early twentieth century, when both rhubarb (cultivated under forcing sheds in the West Yorkshire rhubarb triangle) and ground almonds (available through Victorian-era importing routes) became widely accessible. The pairing appears in Mary Berry's 'The Complete Cookbook' (1995) and in Nigel Slater's 'Tender Volume II' (2010).

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the cooled cake, without icing or dusting, for up to 2 months. Thaw at room temperature for 2 hours.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The cake keeps well for 2 days at room temperature wrapped in foil, and improves slightly on the second day as the rhubarb juice distributes through the crumb.

glossaryTerms.definition: Tossing raw fruit with sugar and leaving it for 20 to 30 minutes so the sugar draws out the fruit's natural juices. In baking, this step removes excess moisture from fruit before it goes into a batter, preventing a wet, sunken patch in the finished cake. The collected juice can be discarded or used in a glaze. For rhubarb specifically, maceration also softens the cell walls slightly, so the pieces become jammy rather than fibrous during the bake.

content.text: Rhubarb releases a large amount of water during baking, which can make the surrounding crumb dense and wet.

before adding it to the batter removes the first wave of this moisture. Toss the chopped rhubarb with sugar and leave it while you make the batter, then drain it well before pressing it into the cake.

Use the most slender stalks of rhubarb you can find, cut into 3 cm lengths. Forced rhubarb (the pale pink winter variety) is milder and works well here; outdoor rhubarb (darker, more fibrous, later in the season) gives a sharper result and needs no adjustment to the recipe.

items.prepNote: trimmed and cut into 3 cm pieces

content.text: Toss the 300 g rhubarb pieces with 50 g caster sugar. Leave in a colander over a bowl for 25 to 30 minutes, then drain away the collected juice.

Heat the oven to 170 degrees C fan. Grease and line a 23 cm round cake tin. Beat the 150 g butter and 175 g caster sugar together until pale and fluffy, about 3 minutes. Beat in the 3 eggs one at a time, adding 1 tbsp flour with each egg to prevent curdling. Stir in the vanilla and almond extracts. Fold in the remaining flour, 100 g ground almonds and 1.5 tsp baking powder.

Spread two thirds of the batter into the prepared tin. Arrange the drained rhubarb pieces over the top, then spoon the remaining batter over them in small blobs, leaving some rhubarb visible. Scatter with 30 g flaked almonds. Bake for 45 to 55 minutes until golden brown on top, the edges are pulling from the tin, and a skewer inserted in the centre comes out clean. Cool in the tin for 15 minutes, then on a rack. Dust with icing sugar before serving.

items.problem: Wet, sunken patch around the rhubarb
items.solution: The rhubarb was not drained thoroughly enough, or the maceration step was skipped. The rhubarb must be drained in a colander for the full 25 to 30 minutes; pressing it lightly with the back of a spoon before adding it to the batter removes additional liquid.

items.problem: Top browns before the centre is set
items.solution: Lay a sheet of foil loosely over the tin after 30 minutes and continue baking until the skewer test passes. The ground almonds make this cake denser than a standard sponge and it needs the full baking time.

items.problem: Rhubarb pieces sink to the base
items.solution: The batter was too loose. Toss the drained rhubarb in a teaspoon of flour before adding it, which helps the pieces stay suspended in the batter during baking.
